summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the most critical factor for virtual machine performance is running VMs from a separate physical hard drive rather than the same drive as your operating system. The disk I/O bottleneck is by far the biggest performance penalty in virtualization, as VM disk images routinely grow to multiple gigabytes. He recommends desktop users adopt a two-drive approach: a fast 10,000 rpm drive for the OS and a separate data drive for VMs. For laptop users, external USB 2.0 or eSATA enclosures provide a practical alternative that delivers noticeable real-world performance improvements.
key_points: Disk I/O is the primary performance bottleneck in virtual machines by a wide margin, not CPU or memory
Running VMs on the same drive as your OS forces them to compete for disk access, significantly degrading performance
A two-drive desktop setup with a fast OS drive and separate data drive is ideal for VM performance
External USB 2.0 enclosures with 7,200 rpm drives can outperform internal 5,400 rpm laptop drives for VM workloads
eSATA connections offer native SATA speeds and are becoming more common on motherboards
money_quotes: The primary performance bottleneck in virtual machines, by a very wide margin, is the hard drive.
Making the VM fight with your OS and swap disk makes this issue much, much worse.
It shouldn't be too surprising that the disk subsystem has a disproportionately large impact on overall virtual machine performance.
The real-world performance improvement gained from running a VM on an external USB 2.0 drive is quite noticeable.
It's also a good way to segregate your operating system and data in case you need to do a complete wipe of your operating system.
key_insight: Virtual machine performance is dominated by disk I/O, so physically separating your VM storage from your OS drive eliminates the contention that causes the biggest slowdowns.
